result:
The most radical act of hope I know is planting a tree. You dig a hole in the indifferent earth, cradle a fragile network of roots, and backfill the soil, knowing you will likely never sit in its shade. You are betting on a future you cannot see, for people you may never meet. It is a quiet argument against despair, a living monument to the belief that tomorrow will come and deserve to be beautiful.

This logic extends beyond the physical. We plant ideas in conversations, kindness in brief interactions, and effort into projects whose full harvest we may not witness. We write letters, mend friendships, and learn difficult things, investing in a richer, more understanding world that is still unfolding. In an age of instant gratification, these are slow, patient investments in time itself.

It feels counterintuitive. We are wired for immediate reward, yet our deepest legacies are almost always delayed. The book that changes a life, the lesson that finally clicks, the oak tree that becomes a landmark—all require the faith that growth happens unseen. To plant is to confess that you are part of a continuum, a link between what was and what will be. It is to say, “I believe in the *after*, and I will leave something good there.” So, I keep planting. Not because I am certain of the weather, but because the act itself is a kind of certainty. It is my stubborn, dirt-under-the-nails declaration that the story continues, and I will help it along.

This logic extends beyond the physical. We plant ideas in conversations, kindness in brief interactions, and effort into projects whose full harvest we may not witness. We write letters, mend friendships, and learn difficult things, investing in a richer, more understanding world that is still unfolding. In an age of instant gratification, these are slow, patient investments in time itself.

It feels counterintuitive. We are wired for immediate reward, yet our deepest legacies are almost always delayed. The book that changes a life, the lesson that finally clicks, the oak tree that becomes a landmark—all require the faith that growth happens unseen. To plant is to confess that you are part of a continuum, a link between what was and what will be. It is to say, “I believe in the *after*, and I will leave something good there.” So, I keep planting. Not because I am certain of the weather, but because the act itself is a kind of certainty.
